Indicator text (no examples): investigates, describes and interprets data collected over time
Example / elaboration: e.g. uses a travel graph to describe a journey; interprets data collected over a period of time using a graphical representation and makes a prediction for the future behaviour of the data)

Indicator text (verbatim): describes everyday occurrences that involve chance (e.g. chance of it raining tomorrow, choosing a name from a hat, making it to the grand final)
Indicator text (no examples): describes everyday occurrences that involve chance
Example / elaboration: e.g. chance of it raining tomorrow, choosing a name from a hat, making it to the grand final)

Indicator text (verbatim): makes predictions on the likelihood of simple, everyday occurrences as to it will or won’t, might or might not happen, based on experiences (e.g. “the plant will die if we don’t water it”,  “next year I will be … years old”; “my tower might not fall down if I add one more brick but it won’t reach the roof”, “we might see a pelican at the lake”)
Indicator text (no examples): makes predictions on the likelihood of simple, everyday occurrences as to it will or won’t, might or might not happen, based on experiences
Example / elaboration: e.g. “the plant will die if we don’t water it”,  “next year I will be … years old”; “my tower might not fall down if I add one more brick but it won’t reach the roof”, “we might see a pelican at the lake”)

Indicator text (verbatim): describes and orders the likelihood of events in non-quantitative terms such as certain, likely, highly likely, unlikely, impossible (e.g. “if there are more blue than red marbles in a bag, blue is more likely to be selected”; “I am certain that I won’t win the competition because I didn’t enter”)
Indicator text (no examples): describes and orders the likelihood of events in non-quantitative terms such as certain, likely, highly likely, unlikely, impossible
Example / elaboration: e.g. “if there are more blue than red marbles in a bag, blue is more likely to be selected”; “I am certain that I won’t win the competition because I didn’t enter”)

Indicator text (verbatim): records outcomes of chance experiments in tables and charts
Indicator text (no examples): records outcomes of chance experiments in tables and charts

Indicator text (verbatim): demonstrates that outcomes of chance experiments may differ from expected results (e.g. “we will not get the same results every time we roll a dice”)
Indicator text (no examples): demonstrates that outcomes of chance experiments may differ from expected results
Example / elaboration: e.g. “we will not get the same results every time we roll a dice”)

Indicator text (verbatim): draws conclusions that recognise variation in results of chance experiments (e.g. “you rolled a lot of sixes this game, I hope I get more sixes next time”)
Indicator text (no examples): draws conclusions that recognise variation in results of chance experiments
Example / elaboration: e.g. “you rolled a lot of sixes this game, I hope I get more sixes next time”)

Indicator text (verbatim): identifies all possible outcomes of one-step experiments and records outcomes in tables and charts
Indicator text (no examples): identifies all possible outcomes of one-step experiments and records outcomes in tables and charts

Indicator text (verbatim): explains why outcomes of chance experiments may differ from expected results (e.g. “just because there are 6 numbers on a dice doesn’t mean you are going to roll a 6 every 6 rolls, you may not roll a 6 in the entire game”)
Indicator text (no examples): explains why outcomes of chance experiments may differ from expected results
Example / elaboration: e.g. “just because there are 6 numbers on a dice doesn’t mean you are going to roll a 6 every 6 rolls, you may not roll a 6 in the entire game”)

Indicator text (verbatim): explains the difference between the notion of equal likelihood of possible outcomes and those that are not equally likely (e.g. explains the use of phrases such as fifty-fifty when there are 2 outcomes and when 2 events occurring are equally likely, as opposed to head and tail are more likely than 2 heads or 2 tails)
Indicator text (no examples): explains the difference between the notion of equal likelihood of possible outcomes and those that are not equally likely
Example / elaboration: e.g. explains the use of phrases such as fifty-fifty when there are 2 outcomes and when 2 events occurring are equally likely, as opposed to head and tail are more likely than 2 heads or 2 tails)

Indicator text (verbatim): identifies unfair elements in games that affect the chances of winning (e.g. having an unequal number of turns; weighted dice)
Indicator text (no examples): identifies unfair elements in games that affect the chances of winning
Example / elaboration: e.g. having an unequal number of turns; weighted dice)

Indicator text (verbatim): explains that the outcomes of chance events are either “certain to happen”, “certain not to happen” or lie somewhere in between and knows that impossible events are events that are “certain not to happen”
Indicator text (no examples): explains that the outcomes of chance events are either “certain to happen”, “certain not to happen” or lie somewhere in between and knows that impossible events are events that are “certain not to happen”

Indicator text (verbatim): identifies events where the chance of one event occurring will not affect the occurrence of the other (e.g. if a coin is tossed and heads have come up 7 times in a row, it is still equally likely that the next toss will be either a head or a tail)
Indicator text (no examples): identifies events where the chance of one event occurring will not affect the occurrence of the other
Example / elaboration: e.g. if a coin is tossed and heads have come up 7 times in a row, it is still equally likely that the next toss will be either a head or a tail)

Indicator text (verbatim): expresses the theoretical probability of an event as the number of ways an event can happen out of the total number of possibilities
Indicator text (no examples): expresses the theoretical probability of an event as the number of ways an event can happen out of the total number of possibilities

Indicator text (verbatim): identifies a range of chance events that have a probability from 0 – 1 (e.g. you have zero probability of rolling a 7 with one roll of a standard 6-sided dice; the probability that tomorrow is Wednesday given today is Tuesday is 1)
Indicator text (no examples): identifies a range of chance events that have a probability from 0 – 1
Example / elaboration: e.g. you have zero probability of rolling a 7 with one roll of a standard 6-sided dice; the probability that tomorrow is Wednesday given today is Tuesday is 1)

Indicator text (verbatim): describes probabilities as fractions of one (e.g. the probability of an even number when rolling a dice is 3/6 )
Indicator text (no examples): describes probabilities as fractions of one
Example / elaboration: e.g. the probability of an even number when rolling a dice is 3/6 )

Indicator text (verbatim): expresses probabilities as fractions, decimals, percentages and ratios recognising that all probabilities lie on a measurement scale of zero to one (e.g. uses numerical representations such as 75% chance of rain or 4 out 5 people liked the story; explains why you can’t have a probability less than zero)
Indicator text (no examples): expresses probabilities as fractions, decimals, percentages and ratios recognising that all probabilities lie on a measurement scale of zero to one
Example / elaboration: e.g. uses numerical representations such as 75% chance of rain or 4 out 5 people liked the story; explains why you can’t have a probability less than zero)

Indicator text (verbatim): determines the probability of compound events and explains why some results have a higher probability than others (e.g. the results from tossing 2 coins)
Indicator text (no examples): determines the probability of compound events and explains why some results have a higher probability than others
Example / elaboration: e.g. the results from tossing 2 coins)

Indicator text (verbatim): represents diagrammatically all possible outcomes (e.g. tree diagrams, two-way tables, Venn diagrams)
Indicator text (no examples): represents diagrammatically all possible outcomes
Example / elaboration: e.g. tree diagrams, two-way tables, Venn diagrams)

Indicator text (verbatim): measures and compares expected results to the actual results of a chance event over a number of trials, and compares and explains the variation in results (e.g. uses probability to determine expected results of a spinner prior to trial)
Indicator text (no examples): measures and compares expected results to the actual results of a chance event over a number of trials, and compares and explains the variation in results
Example / elaboration: e.g. uses probability to determine expected results of a spinner prior to trial)

Indicator text (verbatim): recognises that the chance of something occurring or its complement has a total probability of one (e.g. the probability of rolling a 3 is 1/6 and the probability of not rolling a 3 is 5/6 )
Indicator text (no examples): recognises that the chance of something occurring or its complement has a total probability of one
Example / elaboration: e.g. the probability of rolling a 3 is 1/6 and the probability of not rolling a 3 is 5/6 )

Indicator text (verbatim): calculates and explains the difference between the probabilities of chance events with and without replacement (e.g. “if we put all of the class names in a hat and draw them out one at a time without putting the name back in, the probability of your name getting called out increases each time because the total number of possible outcomes decreases”)
Indicator text (no examples): calculates and explains the difference between the probabilities of chance events with and without replacement
Example / elaboration: e.g. “if we put all of the class names in a hat and draw them out one at a time without putting the name back in, the probability of your name getting called out increases each time because the total number of possible outcomes decreases”)

Indicator text (verbatim): calculates the probabilities of future events based on historical data (e.g. uses historical rainfall data to plan the date for an outdoor event)
Indicator text (no examples): calculates the probabilities of future events based on historical data
Example / elaboration: e.g. uses historical rainfall data to plan the date for an outdoor event)

Indicator text (verbatim): recognises combinations of events and the impact they have on assigning probabilities (e.g. and, or, not, if not, at least)
Indicator text (no examples): recognises combinations of events and the impact they have on assigning probabilities
Example / elaboration: e.g. and, or, not, if not, at least)

Indicator text (verbatim): solves conditional probability problems informally using data in two-way tables and authentic contexts
Indicator text (no examples): solves conditional probability problems informally using data in two-way tables and authentic contexts

Indicator text (verbatim): evaluates chance data reported in media for meaning and accuracy
Indicator text (no examples): evaluates chance data reported in media for meaning and accuracy

Indicator text (verbatim): applies probabilistic/chance reasoning to data collected in statistical investigations when making decisions acknowledging uncertainty
Indicator text (no examples): applies probabilistic/chance reasoning to data collected in statistical investigations when making decisions acknowledging uncertainty

Indicator text (verbatim): poses and answers simple questions and collects responses (e.g. collects data from a simple yes/no question by getting respondents to form a line depending upon their answer)
Indicator text (no examples): poses and answers simple questions and collects responses
Example / elaboration: e.g. collects data from a simple yes/no question by getting respondents to form a line depending upon their answer)

Indicator text (verbatim): displays information using real objects, drawings or photographs (e.g. collects leaves from outside the classroom and displays them in order of size)
Indicator text (no examples): displays information using real objects, drawings or photographs
Example / elaboration: e.g. collects leaves from outside the classroom and displays them in order of size)

Indicator text (verbatim): sorts and classifies shapes and objects into groups based on their features or characteristics and describes how they have been sorted (e.g. sorts objects by colour)
Indicator text (no examples): sorts and classifies shapes and objects into groups based on their features or characteristics and describes how they have been sorted
Example / elaboration: e.g. sorts objects by colour)

Indicator text (verbatim): identifies things that vary or stay the same in everyday life (e.g. “it is always dark at night”; “although jellybeans are the same size, they can be different colours”)
Indicator text (no examples): identifies things that vary or stay the same in everyday life
Example / elaboration: e.g. “it is always dark at night”; “although jellybeans are the same size, they can be different colours”)

Indicator text (verbatim): poses questions that could be investigated from a simple numerical or categorical data set (e.g. number of family members, types of pets, where people live)
Indicator text (no examples): poses questions that could be investigated from a simple numerical or categorical data set
Example / elaboration: e.g. number of family members, types of pets, where people live)

Indicator text (verbatim): displays and describes one-variable data in lists or tables
Indicator text (no examples): displays and describes one-variable data in lists or tables

Indicator text (verbatim): communicates information through text, picture graphs and tables, using numbers and symbols (e.g. creates picture graphs to display one-variable data)
Indicator text (no examples): communicates information through text, picture graphs and tables, using numbers and symbols
Example / elaboration: e.g. creates picture graphs to display one-variable data)

Indicator text (verbatim): responds to questions and interprets general observations made about data represented in simple one-to-one data displays (e.g. responds to questions about the information represented in a simple picture graph that uses a one-to-one representation)
Indicator text (no examples): responds to questions and interprets general observations made about data represented in simple one-to-one data displays
Example / elaboration: e.g. responds to questions about the information represented in a simple picture graph that uses a one-to-one representation)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying and interpreting categorical data
Indicator text (verbatim): designs simple survey questions to collect categorical data (e.g. creates a suite of survey questions to plan the end of year class party)
Indicator text (no examples): designs simple survey questions to collect categorical data
Example / elaboration: e.g. creates a suite of survey questions to plan the end of year class party)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying and interpreting categorical data
Indicator text (verbatim): collects, records and displays one-variable data in variety of way such as tables, charts, plots and graphs using the appropriate digital tools (e.g. uses a spreadsheet to record data collected in a class survey and generates a column graph to display the results)
Indicator text (no examples): collects, records and displays one-variable data in variety of way such as tables, charts, plots and graphs using the appropriate digital tools
Example / elaboration: e.g. uses a spreadsheet to record data collected in a class survey and generates a column graph to display the results)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying and interpreting categorical data
Indicator text (verbatim): displays and interprets categorical data in one-to-many data displays
Indicator text (no examples): displays and interprets categorical data in one-to-many data displays

Subheading: Collecting, displaying and interpreting categorical data
Indicator text (verbatim): interprets and represents categorical data in simple displays such as bar and column graphs, pie charts, models, maps, colour wheels, pictorial timelines, and makes simple inferences from such displays
Indicator text (no examples): interprets and represents categorical data in simple displays such as bar and column graphs, pie charts, models, maps, colour wheels, pictorial timelines, and makes simple inferences from such displays

Subheading: Collecting, displaying and interpreting categorical data
Indicator text (verbatim): makes comparisons from categorical data displays using relative heights from a common baseline (e.g. compares the heights of the columns in a simple column graph to determine the tallest and recognises this as the most frequent response)
Indicator text (no examples): makes comparisons from categorical data displays using relative heights from a common baseline
Example / elaboration: e.g. compares the heights of the columns in a simple column graph to determine the tallest and recognises this as the most frequent response)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying and interpreting numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): collects and records discrete numerical data using an appropriate method for recording (e.g. uses a frequency table to record the experimental results for rolling a dice; records sample measurements taken during a science investigation)
Indicator text (no examples): collects and records discrete numerical data using an appropriate method for recording
Example / elaboration: e.g. uses a frequency table to record the experimental results for rolling a dice; records sample measurements taken during a science investigation)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying and interpreting numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): constructs graphical representations of numerical data and explains the difference between continuous and discrete data (e.g. explains that measurements such as length, mass and temperature are continuous data whereas a count such as the number of people in a queue is discrete)
Indicator text (no examples): constructs graphical representations of numerical data and explains the difference between continuous and discrete data
Example / elaboration: e.g. explains that measurements such as length, mass and temperature are continuous data whereas a count such as the number of people in a queue is discrete)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying and interpreting numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): explains how data displays can be misleading (e.g. whether a scale should start at zero; not using uniform intervals on the axes)
Indicator text (no examples): explains how data displays can be misleading
Example / elaboration: e.g. whether a scale should start at zero; not using uniform intervals on the axes)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying and interpreting numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): interprets visual representations of data displayed using a multi-unit scale, reading values between the marked units and describing any variation and trends in the data
Indicator text (no examples): interprets visual representations of data displayed using a multi-unit scale, reading values between the marked units and describing any variation and trends in the data

Subheading: Collecting, displaying, interpreting and analysing numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): poses questions based on variations in continuous numerical data and chooses the appropriate method to collect and record data (e.g. collects information on the heights of buildings or daily temperatures, tabulates the results and represents these graphically; uses a survey to collect primary data or secondary data extracted from census data)
Indicator text (no examples): poses questions based on variations in continuous numerical data and chooses the appropriate method to collect and record data
Example / elaboration: e.g. collects information on the heights of buildings or daily temperatures, tabulates the results and represents these graphically; uses a survey to collect primary data or secondary data extracted from census data)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying, interpreting and analysing numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): uses numerical and graphical representations relevant to the purpose of the collection of the data and explains their reasoning (e.g. “I can’t use a frequency histogram for categorical data because there is no numerical connection between the categories”; converts their data to percentages in order to compare the girls’ results to those of the boys, as the total number of boys and girls who participated in the survey was different)
Indicator text (no examples): uses numerical and graphical representations relevant to the purpose of the collection of the data and explains their reasoning
Example / elaboration: e.g. “I can’t use a frequency histogram for categorical data because there is no numerical connection between the categories”; converts their data to percentages in order to compare the girls’ results to those of the boys, as the total number of boys and girls who participated in the survey was different)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying, interpreting and analysing numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): determines and calculates the most appropriate statistic to describe the spread of data (e.g. when creating an infographic, uses the mean of the data to describe household income and the median of the data for house prices)
Indicator text (no examples): determines and calculates the most appropriate statistic to describe the spread of data
Example / elaboration: e.g. when creating an infographic, uses the mean of the data to describe household income and the median of the data for house prices)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying, interpreting and analysing numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): calculates simple descriptive statistics such as mode, mean or median as measures to represent typical values of a distribution (e.g. describes the mean kilojoule intake and median hours of exercise of a sample population when investigating community health and wellbeing; describes central tendency when analysing road safety statistics)
Indicator text (no examples): calculates simple descriptive statistics such as mode, mean or median as measures to represent typical values of a distribution
Example / elaboration: e.g. describes the mean kilojoule intake and median hours of exercise of a sample population when investigating community health and wellbeing; describes central tendency when analysing road safety statistics)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying, interpreting and analysing numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): compares the usefulness of different representations of the same data (e.g. chooses to use a line graph to illustrate trends, a bar graph to compare the living standards of different economies or a histogram to show income distribution)
Indicator text (no examples): compares the usefulness of different representations of the same data
Example / elaboration: e.g. chooses to use a line graph to illustrate trends, a bar graph to compare the living standards of different economies or a histogram to show income distribution)

Subheading: Collecting, displaying, interpreting and analysing numerical data
Indicator text (verbatim): describes the spread of a data distribution in terms of the range, clusters, skewness and symmetry of the graphical display, and determines and makes connections to the mode, median and mean of the data
Indicator text (no examples): describes the spread of a data distribution in terms of the range, clusters, skewness and symmetry of the graphical display, and determines and makes connections to the mode, median and mean of the data

Indicator text (verbatim): uses features of graphical representations to make predictions (e.g. predicts audience numbers based on historical data; interprets a range of graphs to identify possible trends and make predictions such as economic growth, stock prices, interest rates, population growth)
Indicator text (no examples): uses features of graphical representations to make predictions
Example / elaboration: e.g. predicts audience numbers based on historical data; interprets a range of graphs to identify possible trends and make predictions such as economic growth, stock prices, interest rates, population growth)

Indicator text (verbatim): summarises data using fractions, percentages and decimals (e.g. of a class live in the same suburb; represents road safety and sun safety statistics as a percentage of the Australian population)
Indicator text (no examples): summarises data using fractions, percentages and decimals
Example / elaboration: e.g. of a class live in the same suburb; represents road safety and sun safety statistics as a percentage of the Australian population)

Indicator text (verbatim): explains that continuous variables depicting growth or change often vary over time (e.g. creates growth charts to illustrate impacts of financial decisions; describes patterns in inflation rates, employment rates, migration rates over time; represents changes to fitness levels following the implementation of a personal fitness plan; interprets temperature charts)
Indicator text (no examples): explains that continuous variables depicting growth or change often vary over time
Example / elaboration: e.g. creates growth charts to illustrate impacts of financial decisions; describes patterns in inflation rates, employment rates, migration rates over time; represents changes to fitness levels following the implementation of a personal fitness plan; interprets temperature charts)

Indicator text (verbatim): interprets graphs depicting motion such as distance–time and velocity–time graphs
Indicator text (no examples): interprets graphs depicting motion such as distance–time and velocity–time graphs

Indicator text (verbatim): interprets and describes patterns in graphical representations of data from real-life situations such as the motion of a rollercoaster, flight trajectory of a basketball shot and the spread of disease
Indicator text (no examples): interprets and describes patterns in graphical representations of data from real-life situations such as the motion of a rollercoaster, flight trajectory of a basketball shot and the spread of disease

Indicator text (verbatim): investigates the association of 2 numerical variables through the representation and interpretation of bivariate data (e.g. uses scatter plots to represent bivariate data when investigating the relationship between 2 variables, such as income per capita, population density and life expectancy for different socio-economic groups)
Indicator text (no examples): investigates the association of 2 numerical variables through the representation and interpretation of bivariate data
Example / elaboration: e.g. uses scatter plots to represent bivariate data when investigating the relationship between 2 variables, such as income per capita, population density and life expectancy for different socio-economic groups)

Indicator text (verbatim): investigates, represents and interprets time series data (e.g. interrogates a time series graph showing the change in costs over time; uses a maximum daily temperature chart to determine the average temperature for the month)
Indicator text (no examples): investigates, represents and interprets time series data
Example / elaboration: e.g. interrogates a time series graph showing the change in costs over time; uses a maximum daily temperature chart to determine the average temperature for the month)

Indicator text (verbatim): interprets the impact of changes to data (e.g. recognises the impact of outliers on a data set such as the income of a world-class professional athlete on the average income of players at the state/territory level; uses digital tools to enhance the quality of data in a science investigation)
Indicator text (no examples): interprets the impact of changes to data
Example / elaboration: e.g. recognises the impact of outliers on a data set such as the income of a world-class professional athlete on the average income of players at the state/territory level; uses digital tools to enhance the quality of data in a science investigation)

Indicator text (verbatim): considers the context when determining whether to use data from a sample or a population
Indicator text (no examples): considers the context when determining whether to use data from a sample or a population

Indicator text (verbatim): determines what type of sample to use from a population (e.g. decides to use a representative sample when conducting targeted market research or when researching beliefs about a health-related issue)
Indicator text (no examples): determines what type of sample to use from a population
Example / elaboration: e.g. decides to use a representative sample when conducting targeted market research or when researching beliefs about a health-related issue)

Indicator text (verbatim): makes reasonable statements about a population based on evidence from samples (e.g. considers accuracy of representation of marginalised individuals or population groups)
Indicator text (no examples): makes reasonable statements about a population based on evidence from samples
Example / elaboration: e.g. considers accuracy of representation of marginalised individuals or population groups)

Indicator text (verbatim): plans, executes and reports on sampling-based investigations, taking into account validity of methodology and consistency of data, to answer questions formulated by the student
Indicator text (no examples): plans, executes and reports on sampling-based investigations, taking into account validity of methodology and consistency of data, to answer questions formulated by the student

Indicator text (verbatim): applies an understanding of distributions to evaluate claims based on data (e.g.  recognises that the accuracy of using a sample for predicting population values depends on both the relative size of the sample and how well the characteristics of the sample reflect the characteristics of the population; critically analyses statistics that reinforce stereotypes; evaluates claims made by the media regarding young people in relation to drugs and/or risk-taking behaviours)
Indicator text (no examples): applies an understanding of distributions to evaluate claims based on data

Indicator text (verbatim): identifies and explains bias as a possible source of error in media reports of survey data (e.g. uses data to evaluate veracity of review headlines such as “everybody’s favourite game”; investigates media claims on attitudes to government responses to market failure or income redistribution)
Indicator text (no examples): identifies and explains bias as a possible source of error in media reports of survey data

Indicator text (verbatim): justifies criticisms of data sources that include biased statistical elements (e.g. inappropriate sampling from populations; identifies sources of uncertainty in a scientific investigation; checks the authenticity of a data set)
Indicator text (no examples): justifies criticisms of data sources that include biased statistical elements

Indicator text (verbatim): estimates and rounds natural numbers to the nearest 10 or nearest 100 (e.g. pencils come in a pack of 10, estimate the number of packs required for 127 Year 6 students; to check the reasonableness of their solution to the computation 212 + 195, rounds both numbers to 200)
Indicator text (no examples): estimates and rounds natural numbers to the nearest 10 or nearest 100

Indicator text (no examples): represents and names tenths as one out of 10 equal parts of a whole

Indicator text (verbatim): represents and names one-tenth as its decimal equivalent 0.1, zero point one
Indicator text (no examples): represents and names one-tenth as its decimal equivalent 0.1, zero point one

Indicator text (verbatim): extends the place value system to tenths
Indicator text (no examples): extends the place value system to tenths

Indicator text (no examples): identifies, reads, writes and interprets numerals beyond 1000, applying knowledge of place value, including numerals that contain a zero

Indicator text (verbatim): estimates and rounds natural numbers to the nearest 10 or nearest 100 (e.g. pencils come in a pack of 10, estimate the number of packs required for 127 Year 6 students; to check the reasonableness of their solution to the computation 212 + 195, rounds both numbers to 200)
Indicator text (no examples): estimates and rounds natural numbers to the nearest 10 or nearest 100

Indicator text (verbatim): represents and names tenths as one out of 10 equal parts of a whole (e.g. uses a bar model to represent the whole and its parts; uses a straw that has been cut into 10 equal pieces to demonstrate that one piece is one-tenth of a whole straw and 2 pieces are two-tenths of the whole straw)
Indicator text (no examples): represents and names tenths as one out of 10 equal parts of a whole

Indicator text (verbatim): represents and names one-tenth as its decimal equivalent 0.1, zero point one extends the place value system to tenths
Indicator text (no examples): represents and names one-tenth as its decimal equivalent 0.1, zero point one extends the place value system to tenths

Indicator text (verbatim): identifies, reads, writes and interprets numerals, beyond 4 digits in length, with spacing after every 3 digits (e.g. 10 204, 25 000 000; 12 230.25; reads 152 450 as “one hundred and 52 thousand 4 hundred and 50”; compares the size of populations for different countries or the cost of expensive items with an advertised selling price in the millions)
